---
title: "02_TimeAlignment"
author: "Rhiannon Nolan"
date: "2026-04-14"
output: html_document
---

```{r setup, include=FALSE}
knitr::opts_chunk$set(echo = F)
```

```{r}
#install necessary packages
if (!require(rstudioapi)){install.packages("rstudioapi")}
me <- (rstudioapi::getActiveDocumentContext()$path) #get current file name
wd <- dirname(me)

if (!require(renv)){ install.packages("renv")} #use renv to find package dependencies
if(!require(iNEXT)){remotes::install_version("iNEXT", version = "2.0.20")} #because I use an old version of iNEXT
packs <- unique(renv::dependencies(me)$Package) #list of packages needed for this document
for (i in seq_along(packs)){
  if (!require(packs[i], character.only=T)){ #install any missing packs
    install.packages(packs[i])
  }
}
lapply(packs, library, character.only=T) #load packages
rm(me, i) #clean env
```

### This markdown assumes you've run the code in 01_Data_Compilation.Rmd

```{r}
load(paste0(wd, "/Data/comp_dat.RData"))
```

```{r}
#Hay, C.C., et al., 2019, A library of early Cambrian chemostratigraphic correlations from a reproducible algorithm: Geology, v. 47, p. 457–460, https://doi.org/10.1130/G46019.1
#Using algorythm to align columns 

#They use a complex version of dynamic time warping. For this project, I use a simplified dtw using the r package 'dtw'
library(dtw)
#https://dynamictimewarping.github.io/

alignment <- list() #list in which i will put dtw objects


for( i in seq_along (test_data)){ #start out with for loop going through each of my sections
  og_name <- names(test_data)[i] #section in my data
  y <- test_data[[i]]$d13C #d13C data that i'm aligning to the dated columns
  
  for (j in seq_along(align_data)){ #for loop going through each of the reference sections
    x <- align_data[[j]]$d13C #d13C that is the reference to which my data is aligned
    name <- paste0(og_name, "_", names(align_data)[j])
    
    wiggle <- dtw(x = x, y = y, #compute alignment using dtw
                  step.pattern = asymmetric,
                  open.end = T,
                  open.begin = T)
    
    alignment[[name]] <- wiggle
    
  }
  
}

```

```{r}
#integrate the different alignments to find the best one for each section
names(test_data) #these are the sections I tested

#the dtw object creates indices where x[i] = y[j] so the two columns can be aligned

CP1 <- alignment[1:18]
plot(CP1[[1]]$index1,CP1[[1]]$index2 )

#i want to use all the alignments to make one mega one
#so i need a way to combine all my dated columns usefully
#and then extract index 1 and 2 for each alignmenmt and use those for each section

```

```{r}
#Hay, C.C., et al., 2019, A library of early Cambrian chemostratigraphic correlations from a reproducible algorithm: Geology, v. 47, p. 457–460, https://doi.org/10.1130/G46019.1
#Using algorythm to align columns 

#They use a complex version of dynamic time warping. For this project, I use a simplified dtw using the r package 'dtw'
library(dtw)
#https://dynamictimewarping.github.io/

#step-through from demo(dtw)
#set up example using my data from the Fault and Sosa Montes de Oca data from TCC
query <- mydata$d.13C.12C..VPDB[mydata$Section == "TCC-F"]
template <- SMdO$d13C[SMdO$Section == "TCC"]

dtw(query,template,keep=TRUE)->alignment #make alignment object
plot(alignment$index1,alignment$index2) #display mapping
plot(alignment,xts=query,yts=template,type="threeway") #three way plot

## A profile of the cumulative distance matrix
plot(alignment,type="density")

# Do the same with asymmetric step
dtw(query,template,keep=TRUE,step=asymmetric)->ita
plot(ita,type="density",main="TCC Fault and TCC Cumulative, asymmetric step")

 ## Windowing functions (global constraints) can be applied and plotted
dtwWindow.plot(itakuraWindow, main="So-called Itakura parallelogram window")

## Symmetric step with global parallelogram-shaped constraint
## Note how long (>2 steps) horizontal stretches are allowed within the window.
dtw(query,template,keep=TRUE,window=itakuraWindow)->ita;
dtwPlot(ita,type="density",main="Symmetric step with Itakura parallelogram window")

## Asymmetric step with slope constraint
## The "Itakura parallelogram" arises from the local constraint
## plus the boundary condition. Three sides of the parallelogram are seen
dtw(query,template,keep=TRUE,step=typeIIIc)->ita;
dtwPlot(ita,type="density",main="Slope-limited asymmetric step (Itakura)");

## Local and global constraint can be in effect at the same time
## Sakoe-chiba band, plus asymmetric step pattern
asyband<-dtw(query,template,keep=TRUE,
              step=asymmetric,
              window.type=sakoeChibaWindow,
              window.size=30                  );

dtwPlot(asyband,type="density",main="Sine/cosine: asymmetric step, S-C window")

```
